description: Create a content slide in a three-column layout on how foundational models are transforming robots, with narrative text sections for contextual understanding, communication, and planning and action, plus a pull-quote on the right.

header.kicker: Technology Vision 2025 | AI: A Declaration of Autonomy
header.headline: When LLMs get their Bodies

items.text: Businesses in every industry need to imagine what their business could become in a world where robots are accessible, flexible, and effectively think for themselves, enabling OT to tap into AI autonomy.
Foundational models are transforming robots across three dimensions: contextual understanding, communication, and planning and action.
Robots are getting better not just at recognizing but understanding things, linking information from the internet to the physical world so they can intervene or interact appropriately.

Organizations are building systems and datasets to support this, including Stanford University and Robotics at Google with the PhysObjects dataset of hundreds of thousands of annotations on physical concepts and properties.
Researchers are also improving robots’ understanding of space; a 2024 navigation technique combines long-context VLM common-sense reasoning with topological graph-based navigation.

center.title: Evolution #2 Communication / Evolution #3 Planning and Action

items.text: After training on video tours of an office space, robots could maneuver, guide people around, and answer context-based questions such as where to place an item.
Machine communication is moving from lines of code toward natural language, allowing anyone to interact with complex machines.
Robots like Figure 01 and Google Research’s PaLM-E can be instructed in plain speech; in one demo, a mobile robot was asked to bring a bag of chips and planned and executed the required actions.
LLMs combined with sensor data help robots understand spoken commands, devise a plan, and execute it.
Robots can now use LLMs to interpret abstract prompts and break complex tasks into smaller steps, expanding their ability to react to novel commands and situations.
MIT engineers are connecting robot motion data to the common sense of an LLM so robots can break household tasks into subtasks and adjust more flexibly to disruptions.
The combination of these advances is driving the rise of generalist robots and a major step change in robotics.
Every enterprise with physical operations should pay attention, as robotic innovation will reshape how environments, processes, workflows, and physical limits are designed.
Humanoid robot development has accelerated, with deployments moving into warehouses and factories as companies seek bodies to match generalist AI brains.
Goldman Sachs estimates the global market for humanoid robots could reach $38 billion by 2035, pointing to a future where robots are widely capable and adaptable.
While each of these three dimensions alone would be enough to advance robotics, combined they are spurring the biggest step change in robotic history: the rise of generalists.
